w:r.w:t: In India, a typical doctor consultation lasts under 2 minutes. Patients walk out of hospitals holding a discharge summary or prescription filled with medical jargon they cannot read, let alone act on. With no guidance, they turn to Google at 2 AM, land on alarming or wrong information, and either over-medicate or miss critical follow-ups.
Core pain: The gap between what a doctor writes and what a patient understands.
This gap is not a literacy problem — it is a translation problem.
No consumer tool in India today bridges it faithfully and safely.

MedBuddy closes this gap. A patient uploads their prescription or discharge summary and instantly receives a plain-language breakdown of their diagnosis, a clean medication schedule, side effect alerts, and a follow-up checklist — all derived strictly from the uploaded document, with nothing added.

— someone who has just received a hospital discharge summary or prescription and needs to understand and act on it. Typically 25–65 years old, semi-urban or urban India. Comfortable with a smartphone but not with medical terminology.
who rely on a younger family member to decode their documents.
managing a parent or spouse’s treatment at home.
Out of scope for V1: Healthcare professionals, pharmacists, insurance companies, hospital systems.
User uploads a PDF, image (JPG/PNG), or pastes plain text of their prescription or discharge summary.
Optionally enters patient age and preferred output language (English or Hindi).
Clicks ‘Analyse’. System shows a loading state with a progress indicator.
AI parses the document, extracts structured data, and generates all output sections.
Results screen displays: diagnosis summary, medication table, side effect alerts, follow-up checklist, and one-line family summary.
User reads the plain-language diagnosis explanation.
User scans the medication table (medicine name, dosage, timing, duration).
User reads side effect alerts and learns when to call the doctor.
User ticks off follow-up items (tests ordered, diet restrictions, activity limits).
User copies or shares the one-line family summary via WhatsApp or SMS.
User clicks ‘Compare’ to toggle the original jargon text alongside the plain-language output.
Each section of the output is visually paired with its source text from the original document.
User can verify that the explanation matches what the doctor wrote.
Accept PDF, JPG, PNG, and plain text input.
On upload, display a thumbnail or file name for user confirmation before processing.
Patient age field (free-text number) to personalize tone of explanation.
Extract the diagnosis or condition from the document.
Rewrite it in simple, conversational language (no medical jargon).
Must stay strictly faithful to what is written — no interpretation, no additions.
Extract every medicine from the document.
Display as a formatted table: Medicine Name | Dosage | Timing | Duration.
Dosage and timing must match the prescription exactly. Any deviation is a disqualifying patient safety failure.
List the top 2–3 most important things to watch for per the document context.
Include a clear ‘Call the doctor immediately if’ trigger condition.
Derived from the document; do not introduce external medical knowledge.
Extract follow-up tests ordered, diet restrictions, and activity limits.
Render as a simple tick-list the patient can check off.
Generate a single plain sentence the patient can share with a family member.
Must capture diagnosis + key action in under 25 words.
Toggle to show original document text alongside plain-language output.
Ensures transparency and lets clinically literate users verify accuracy.
Drives adherence after the explanation phase
Serves low-literacy users in regional India
Most common sharing channel in India
Let patient ask follow-up questions about their document
Show a warning that the image may be too low quality for accurate parsing. Proceed but flag uncertain fields.
Attempt OCR. If confidence is low, notify the user that handwritten content may contain errors and to verify the medication table manually.
Process all pages; concatenate extracted text before parsing.
Detect absence of medical keywords and show an error: ‘This does not appear to be a prescription or discharge summary.’
Show a clear error immediately on upload, before processing.
Reject immediately with a file size error.
Map to full plain-English equivalents (e.g., BD → twice daily). Use a fixed, validated mapping table.
Render as ‘Not specified’ in the medication table. Never guess or infer.
List each separately in the diagnosis section.
Surface the conflict to the user; do not resolve it arbitrarily.
Process both; output in the user’s chosen language.
AI generates a dosage different from the prescription:
This is an immediate disqualification scenario. The medication table must be extracted, not generated. Use strict extraction prompting.
Prohibited. System prompt must enforce zero external knowledge injection.
Show ‘No follow-up instructions found in document’ rather than a blank section.
The following are explicitly out of scope for V1. Building these would dilute focus and introduce safety risk.
• No alternative medicines or dosage suggestions.
• No symptom checker or diagnostic reasoning beyond the document.
• No integration with external medical databases (drug interaction APIs, etc.).
• No user accounts, login, or persistent data storage.
• No chatbot or free-form medical Q&A.
• No real-time camera capture or live document scanning.
• No support for veterinary or dental prescriptions.
• No export to PDF or print functionality.
Every field in the medication table matches the uploaded prescription exactly. Zero errors tolerated.
Full output generated in under 10 seconds for a standard 1–2 page document.
Diagnosis, medication table, side effect alerts, follow-up checklist, and one-line summary all present.
PDF upload, image upload, and plain text paste all successfully produce output.
Original text and plain-language output are correctly paired and visible.
> 80% of users say they understood their prescription after using MedBuddy
> 85% of uploads result in a complete output (not abandoned or errored)
0% — verified via manual audit of 50 random outputs
The AI must only simplify and extract. It must not add, infer, or substitute any medical information not present in the uploaded document.
The medication table is a patient safety surface. Any incorrect dosage, timing, or medicine name is a direct harm vector.
Every output must include a footer: ‘This summary is generated from your document only. Always follow your doctor’s instructions.’
Documents must not be stored or logged beyond the session. No PII retention.
System prompt must explicitly prohibit external knowledge, alternative suggestions, and diagnostic reasoning.
Frontend: React or plain HTML/CSS — simple upload UI, results display, side-by-side toggle.
Document parsing: PDF.js (PDF text extraction) + Tesseract.js or cloud OCR (image input).
AI layer: Claude API (multimodal) or GPT-4o with a strict extraction system prompt.
Structured output: Prompt the model to return JSON; render the JSON into UI sections.
Language: Pass the selected language in the system prompt; model handles translation.
Hosting: Vercel or Netlify for rapid hackathon deployment.
MedBuddy • Product Requirements Document • V1.0 • March 2026
